questions.question: What is the primary function of the 'next' pointer in a singly linked list?
options: A) To store the previous node
B) To store the next node
D) To store the size of the list
questions.explanation: The 'next' pointer in a singly linked list is used to point to the next node in the sequence.

questions.question: Which of the following operations is not possible in a singly linked list?
questions.explanation: While singly linked lists can be extended to support deletion at the middle, this operation is generally more complex and is not typically supported in standard singly linked lists.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'self-pointer' in a self-referential linked list?
options: B) To store the size of the list
C) To point to the previous node
D) To point to the next node
questions.explanation: The self-pointer in a self-referential linked list is used to point to the previous node in the sequence.

questions.question: Which of the following operations is typically supported by all linked lists?
questions.explanation: Both insertion at the beginning and insertion at the end are typically supported by all linked lists.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a self-referential linked list?
questions.explanation: Self-referential linked lists can provide better memory utilization, as each node can point to itself, allowing for more efficient use of memory.
